content.problemStatement:
Given a binary tree, print the level order traversal of its nodes. (i.e., from left to right, on each level).

For example, the level order traversal of the following binary tree is: 

1 

2 3 

4 5




Input Format

The first line of the input contains a single integer NNN — the number of nodes in the binary tree.
Next N−1N - 1N−1 lines contain three space separated characters pi,ci,Rip_i, c_i, R_ipi​,ci​,Ri​, describing the tree with edge informations - pip_ipi​ is an integer denoting the parent node of the iiith edge, cic_ici​ is child node, and the letter RiR_iRi​ denotes whether the child is left child or right child, it's value is `L` if cic_ici​ is left child of pip_ipi​, else `R`.


Output Format
Output on the L separate lines, NNN space separated integers - its nodes in the level order traversal of the given tree, where L is total no. of levels in the given tree.
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case: first line contains an integer N, the number of nodes in the binary tree. The next N-1 lines each contain three space‑separated values pi ci Ri where pi is the parent node, ci is the child node and Ri is either 'L' (ci is the left child of pi) or 'R' (ci is the right child of pi).
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print the level order traversal of the tree. Nodes belonging to the same depth are printed on one line separated by a single space. After printing all levels of a test case, print a blank line before the next test case (no extra blank line after the last test case).

sampleTestCases.explanation: The tree root is 1. Level 0 contains 1, level 1 contains 2 and 3, level 2 contains 4 and 5.
Root 1 with left child 2 and right child 3. Levels are printed accordingly.
A single‑node tree consists only of the root.

solution.approach: For each test case we first build the tree. Because node values are from 1 to N we can store pointers in an array of size N+1. While reading the edges we also mark every child node; the node that never appears as a child is the root. After the tree is built we perform a breadth‑first search (BFS) using a queue. By processing the queue level by level we can output all nodes of the same depth on one line. This yields the required level order traversal. The algorithm runs in linear time and uses linear extra memory.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, O(total N) for all test cases combined.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) per test case for storing the tree and the BFS queue.
